title: Goat kid weaning and growth checks
subtitle: Weaning at the right point and confirming the kid is ready.
excerpt: Weaning a goat kid too early leads to check in growth and digestive upset. Too late and the doe's milk production for the season is compromised. A kid ready to wean is eating solid feed confidently, has doubled its birth weight, and is at least eight weeks old. The process itself can be done abruptly or gradually over five to seven days.
sourceNotes: DEFRA Code of Recommendations for the Welfare of Goats. British Goat Society management notes, britishgoatsociety.com. SAC Consulting goat production guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: High-energy compound feed accessible only to young animals through a barrier that adults cannot pass. Used to supplement milk intake and encourage early rumen development.

The process by which a newborn ruminant's rumen grows from a non-functional chamber to the dominant digestive organ. Eating solid fibrous feed accelerates rumen development; a milk-only diet delays it.

content.text: A goat kid's digestive system changes rapidly in the first weeks of life. The

is present at birth but non-functional; the kid is effectively a monogastric for the first few weeks. The introduction of hay and solid feed alongside milk stimulates rumen growth, and by six to eight weeks most kids have sufficient rumen capacity to support themselves on forage.
When a kid is ready to wean
Minimum eight weeks of age. Has been eating solid feed (hay, browse, or
) confidently for at least two weeks. Has doubled its birth weight, a single dairy kid born at 3 kg should be at 6 kg or above before weaning is attempted.
A kid weaned before these thresholds will lose weight. It will also show digestive upset for several days. The rumen is not yet developed enough to replace the nutrition that milk provided.

Separate the kid completely from the doe on a single day. The kid will call for a day or two; the doe will call back and may show a full udder. The separation must be complete, the kid can hear and smell the doe through adjacent fencing, which prolongs distress. Move them to a point where neither can hear the other, at least 50 metres apart.

Abrupt weaning is easier on the management and harder on the animals in the short term. Ensure the kid has ad-lib hay, fresh water, and a companion within twenty-four hours. A solitary kid in a pen for the first week after weaning will show more distress than one in a group.

Over five to seven days, progressively reduce milk access. Separate for increasing periods during the day; allow feeding morning and evening; then evening only; then alternate days; then complete separation. This is gentler on both parties and is the preferred method for single kids.

Weigh the kid one week and four weeks after weaning. A check in growth in the first week is normal; consistent weight gain by week four confirms the transition has gone well. A kid that is still below its weaning weight at four weeks post-weaning needs supplementary feed and investigation of rumen health.

Coccidiosis is the most common post-weaning illness in kid goats. The stress of separation compromises immunity, and coccidial oocysts already present in the environment proliferate. A kid that develops profuse diarrhoea in the two weeks after weaning should be isolated and assessed by a vet.
